question: Honeywell's first-quarter orders rose 7% and backlog approached $38 billion, but reported EPS fell 35% and sales missed expectations.
Progressive's first-quarter net income increased 10% to about $2.8 billion as net premiums earned grew 8% from the prior year.
American Airlines reported record first-quarter revenue of $13.9 billion, but still posted a GAAP net loss and faced a sharply higher fuel bill.
Mercedes-Benz first-quarter operating profit fell 17% as China competition, tariff pressure, and higher input costs weighed on results.
Porsche's first-quarter operating profit dropped 22% as U.S. tariffs, weaker China demand, and Middle East uncertainty pressured margins.
AbbVie topped quarterly revenue and earnings expectations as Skyrizi and Rinvoq growth helped offset the continued Humira decline.
Biogen beat first-quarter profit expectations on strong Leqembi and rare-disease drug demand, but lowered its annual profit forecast because of acquisition-related costs.
Humana beat first-quarter profit estimates, but its unchanged annual forecast disappointed investors concerned about Medicare Advantage cost pressure.
Centene raised its 2026 profit forecast after better medical-cost control and stronger-than-expected quarterly results.
London Stock Exchange Group lifted its 2026 revenue outlook after first-quarter income grew 9.8% on stronger trading activity.
$HON has strong orders and a huge backlog, but the sales miss and lower reported EPS make the quarter feel mixed.
$PGR net income and premiums both rising says the insurer is still executing well.
$AAL record revenue does not matter much if fuel costs are exploding and the company is still losing money.
Mercedes profit falling again while China sales stay weak makes the luxury-auto story look rough.
Porsche's margin getting squeezed by tariffs and weaker demand is not the premium-auto setup investors wanted.
$ABBV getting strong Skyrizi and Rinvoq growth while raising guidance makes the Humira cliff look more manageable.
$BIIB beat the quarter, but cutting the full-year profit guide keeps the story from being fully clean.
$HUM beat Q1, but leaving guidance unchanged with medical-cost pressure still around is not exactly exciting.
$CNC raising its profit forecast after better cost control is the kind of health-insurer update investors want.
$LSEG getting a trading boost and guiding to the high end of revenue growth makes the market-data story look better.
Orders increased 7% and backlog remained strong, although reported earnings declined because of restructuring and impairment charges.
Net premiums earned increased 8%, and first-quarter net income rose 10% compared with the prior year.
Record first-quarter revenue was offset by a GAAP net loss and significantly higher jet-fuel costs.
Operating profit declined as tariff pressure, weaker China demand, and higher input costs weighed on profitability.
Operating margin narrowed during the quarter as lower deliveries, tariffs, and regional demand uncertainty pressured earnings.
Newer immunology medicines continued to grow strongly, helping offset the decline in Humira revenue.
Growth products performed above expectations, but acquisition-related research and development charges reduced our full-year profit outlook.
Medical-cost trends improved in the quarter, but we are maintaining our annual earnings outlook because second-half uncertainty remains elevated.
Improved medical-cost control supported stronger quarterly results and allowed us to raise the full-year adjusted profit outlook.
First-quarter income grew 9.8%, and we now expect annual revenue growth near the upper end of our guidance range.
BTG Pactual Timberland Investment Group raised $1.24 billion for a Latin America reforestation strategy focused on restoring degraded land.
Corporate climate-accounting groups considered diverging Scope 2 emissions rules, creating uncertainty for companies' sustainability reporting.
Global fossil-fuel carbon dioxide emissions were projected to reach a record 38.1 billion metric tons in 2025 despite renewable growth.
New food-sector climate reporting rules raised the standard for regenerative-agriculture claims and land-use emissions disclosures.
World Bank climate-finance targets came under pressure after the U.S. pushed the lender to drop its 45% climate-lending goal.
Rising physical climate risks contributed to higher insurance premiums and cancellations in vulnerable housing markets.
PJM Interconnection reopened processing for new power-plant applications, including battery storage, solar, wind, nuclear, and hydropower projects.
The European Union prepared guidance to reduce dependence on Middle Eastern jet fuel by encouraging sustainable aviation fuel and synthetic fuel options.
European Union countries agreed to keep pushing for a global price on shipping carbon emissions in U.N. talks.
Energy-security concerns after the Iran war increased global interest in solar power and other renewable energy alternatives.
Romania's minority government faced a no-confidence vote that risked policy gridlock and access to more than 10 billion euros in EU recovery funds.
Argentina credit stress deepened as inflation and higher utility costs continued to strain household finances.
Nigeria capped jet-fuel prices after a sharp fuel-cost surge threatened airline schedules and pushed carriers toward higher fares.
Morocco's annual inflation rose to 0.9% in March from negative territory, signaling a mild return of price pressure.
Euro zone business activity unexpectedly contracted in April as surging input costs pulled the composite PMI below 50.
Indonesia was expected to keep interest rates at 4.75% through 2026 as the Iran war increased inflation risks.
Ghana's consumer inflation slowed for a fifteenth straight month in March, moving closer to the central bank's target range.
The Bank of Japan signaled a likely June rate hike as energy shocks pushed its inflation forecasts above target.
The Bank of England was expected to hold rates steady as the Iran war clouded the outlook for inflation and growth.
A global Reuters poll raised 2026 inflation forecasts for 44 of the top 50 economies after the energy-price shock.
